coredata.dc:title: Design of lanthanide-doped carbon dots Nanochemosensor for Paper-strip based as well as in-vitro Turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Amit
Singh, Prabhakar
Kumari, Pooja
Nidhi, Kumari
Singh, Ratnesh Kumar
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
Kumari, Archana
coredata.dc:description: Shifting emission wavelengths through different excitation methods can provide valuable insights; however, the reduced intensity of the shifted peaks often presents challenges in practical applications. In this work, we report the exceptional enhancement of optical and chemical properties of carbon dots (CDs) by simple doping of erbium ions (Er3+ ions). Doping carbon dots (CDs) with Er3+ ions can significantly enhance their photoluminescence properties and emission wavelengths are tunable across the entire visible region. The increase in quantum yield from 24.7 % to 30.2 % could enhance the performance of these materials in practical applications. The Er3+ ions-doped carbon dots (Er@CDs) are designed via a single-step hydrothermal method and used as a fluorescence probe for signal turn-off–on detections of Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The fluorescence signal of Er@CDs is turn-off by the addition of Fe2+ ions and turn-on after addition of H2O2 with the detection of limits are 51.7 nM & 55 µM, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of Er@CDs turn-off by Fe2+ ions may be due to the cation exchange of Er3+ ions with Fe2+ ions and turn-on via Fenton’s process. This sensing probe follows both dynamic and static quenching mechanism and described by fluorescence lifetime decay and zeta potentials analysis. The synthesized Er@CDs is utilized for paper-based turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2. Additionally, the low cytotoxicity and small particle size of the Er@CDs make them ideal for in-vitro turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2 in Hep G2 cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Er@CDs
Turn-off–on Detection
In-vitro Sensing
Visual Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity assay of Fe+2 ions and H2O2
Detection of Fe2+ ions by fluorometric method

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, Jharkhand, India. We also thank the Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur, Uttar Pradesh, India, for providing the research analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission bar diagram of different (a) metal ions, and (b) radicals.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs and Er@CDs, (b) excitation-independent fluorescence spectra of CDs [360–410 nm], (c) excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of Er@CDs [360–570 nm], and (d) normalized emission spectra of Er@CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of CDs & Er@CDs, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s, and (e) Er 3d as well as 4d.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of synthesized CDs & Er@CDs.

TEM image of (a) CDs [particles size distribution diagram (in inset)] & (c) Er@CDs [HRTEM image and particles size distribution diagram (in inset)] and corresponding SAED patterns of (b) CDs and (d) Er@CDs.

(a) fluorescence spectra of Er@CDs in the presence of various concentration (0–10 μM) of Fe2+ ions (b) linear relationship curve between emission intensities and concentration of Fe2+ ions [Stern-Volmer calibration plot in inset], (c) turn-on emission spectra of (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions) by the addition of different concentrations (0–1000 μM) of H2O2, and (d) linear relationship between emission intensities of (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions) and H2O2 concentrations [Stern-Volmer calibration plot in inset].
(a) Fluorescence emission bar diagram of mixed metal ions without Fe2+ ions & with Fe2+ ions, and (b) fluorescence emission bar diagram of mixed radicals in the absence and presence of H2O2.

(a) Paper-based turn-off visual detection of Fe2+ ions with increasing the concentrations [0–10 μM], (b) paper-based turn-on visual detection of H2O2 with increasing the concentrations [0–1000 μM] under UV-light (365 nm), (c) cytotoxicity study of Er@CDs [0–100 µg/mL], and (d) cell cytotoxicity analysis of H2O2 [0–50 µg/mL].
(a) Fluorescence lifetime decay, and (b) zeta potential graph of Er@CDs, [Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions], and [Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions + H2O2], respectively.
In-vitro turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2 in Hep G2 cells at excitation with bright, green [450–480 nm] and, merge field [scale bar 50 μM].
Graphic layout for turn-off–on detection mechanism of Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide by the fabricated Er@CDs.
Illustration for the synthesis process of CDs and Er@CDs by the simple hydrothermal process, and their application for selective turn-off–on detection of Fe+2 ions and H2O2.
The evaluation of the projected technique with other described procedures of hydrogen peroxide determination.

head.ce:title: Design of lanthanide-doped carbon dots Nanochemosensor for Paper-strip based as well as in-vitro Turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Amit Kumar and Prabhakar Singh contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Shifting emission wavelengths through different excitation methods can provide valuable insights; however, the reduced intensity of the shifted peaks often presents challenges in practical applications. In this work, we report the exceptional enhancement of optical and chemical properties of carbon dots (CDs) by simple doping of erbium ions (Er3+ ions). Doping carbon dots (CDs) with Er3+ ions can significantly enhance their photoluminescence properties and emission wavelengths are tunable across the entire visible region. The increase in quantum yield from 24.7 % to 30.2 % could enhance the performance of these materials in practical applications. The Er3+ ions-doped carbon dots (Er@CDs) are designed via a single-step hydrothermal method and used as a fluorescence probe for signal turn-off–on detections of Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The fluorescence signal of Er@CDs is turn-off by the addition of Fe2+ ions and turn-on after addition of H2O2 with the detection of limits are 51.7 nM & 55 µM, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of Er@CDs turn-off by Fe2+ ions may be due to the cation exchange of Er3+ ions with Fe2+ ions and turn-on via Fenton’s process. This sensing probe follows both dynamic and static quenching mechanism and described by fluorescence lifetime decay and zeta potentials analysis. The synthesized Er@CDs is utilized for paper-based turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2. Additionally, the low cytotoxicity and small particle size of the Er@CDs make them ideal for in-vitro turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2 in Hep G2 cancer cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials known for their exceptional optical and chemical properties. The unique optical properties of CDs arise from their ability to absorb and emit light at different wavelengths, while their chemical stability ensures that they maintain performance over time and in diverse conditions [1–5]. In recent year, researchers have increasingly concentrated on enhancing the chemical and optical properties of CDs through precise control of their size and surface passivation [6]. This process can reduce non-radiative recombination and quenching effects, leading to brighter and more stable fluorescence [6–8]. Overall, advancements in controlling CD size and surface characteristics are paving the way for more effective and versatile applications of these nanomaterials in both scientific research and practical applications. Compared with non-metal [6–8], metal ion doping in CDs has proven to be more efficient and convenient in optimizing their optical and chemical properties. Metal ions can significantly influence the electronic structure and photophysical behavior of CDs, leading to enhanced fluorescence intensity, improved photostability, and tunable emission wavelengths [9–11]. The incorporation of metal ions can introduce new energy levels within the band gap of CDs, facilitating improved charge separation and reducing non-radiative decay processes. These results in brighter and more stable fluorescence compared to non-metal dopants, which often have a more limited impact on the electronic structure of CDs [12–14]. Metal ions doping, particularly lanthanide ions (Ln-ions), significantly impacts on the properties of CDs due to their unique atomic structures [15–21]. Ln-ions, such as europium (Eu) [15], terbium (Tb) [16], and gadolinium (Gd) [17], have distinct electronic configurations with partially filled f-orbitals. These f-orbitals are responsible for sharp, well-defined emission lines and long-lived luminescence across the visible to near-infrared spectra [15–21]. Ln-ions typically exhibit low intrinsic fluorescence efficiency due to their electronic structure, where the f-orbitals are deeply buried and transitions between these orbitals have low probability, resulting in weak emission. However, this limitation can be effectively overcome by utilizing the “antenna effect” [20–23]. By carefully selecting and engineering the ligands to match the absorption and emission characteristics of the Ln-ions, researchers can significantly enhance the fluorescence efficiency of the doped CDs [15–20]. This results in bright and intense emission, with well-defined spectral properties. Due to these spectral properties, the doped CDs are useful for a variety of applications, including imaging, sensing, and display technologies [15–23]. In recent years, few Ln-doped CDs have been reported in the field of sensing and imaging. For example, Liu et al. prepared Er@CDs in a one-pot carbonization process to detect tetracycline [22]. Yang et al. enhanced the fluorescence properties of carbon dots by lanthanide ion doping and used for intracellular imaging of ferric ion [16]. Zhang et al. prepared lanthanide-doped carbon dots for mercury ion detection and cell imaging [17]. Chen et al. established terbium-doped CDs for selective sensing of picric acid [18]. Chen et al. synthesized terbium (III) doped CDs for selective and sensitive sensing of stringent [20]. However, it is still required to prepare a rapid and straightforward way to synthesize Ln-doped CDs, which can be used for cellular imaging and turn-off–on detecting probe. There is a high need to design a fluorescence-based chemosensor which can be used for selective turn-off–on detection of ferrous (Fe2+) ions and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), simultaneously.

Iron is the most abundant transition metal of all living organisms. It exists in two stable redox states, ferric (Fe3+) ions and ferrous (Fe2+) ions. Ferrous (Fe2+) ions are more water-soluble than Fe3+ ions. It serves in many biological functions, such as electron carriers, oxygen transport, and storage [24–27]. The redox reaction involving Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide generates hydroxyl radical and other highly reactive oxygen species by the well- known Fenton reaction [5,6]. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a relatively mild reactive oxygen species (ROS) among ROS, which plays a crucial role in cellular signal transduction [28–31]. In the presence of enzymes and O2, many biological materials can be catalyzed and produce H2O2 as a by-product [26–31]. Though, the excessive concentration of H2O2 (0.7 μM) is associated with oxidative stress and a substantial threat to living cells [24–29]. The excess discharge of H2O2 can cause several problems inducing damaging DNA, lipids, proteins, Parkinson’s disease, cancers, and Alzheimer's disease [32–40]. So, sensitive and selective sensing of H2O2 has great importance for environmental, toxicological, and biological diagnosis.

In this work, we have designed a novel fluorescent material by doping CDs with erbium (Er3+) ions to enhance their optical and chemical properties. By strategically doping Er3+ ions into the carbon-core of CDs can improve their optical performance and broadening the potential applications in areas of sensing, bioimaging, and environmental monitoring. This version emphasizes the indirect role of Er3+ ions in enhancing the properties of CDs. Erbium-doped carbon dots (Er@CDs) have been prepared by the hydrothermal process and used for the selective and sensitive “turn-off–on” sensing of Fe2+ ions and H2O2, respectively. Initially, the fluorescence intensities of Er@CDs are quenched by the addition of Fe2+ ions (turn-off) due to the formation of a chelating ring system between Er@CDs and Fe2+ ions, which disrupts the fluorescence emission. However, when H2O2 is added; the fluorescence intensity of Er@CDs is recovered (turn-on). Here, the dramatic recovery of fluorescence intensity of Er@CDs occurs due to H2O2 which can oxidize the Fe2+ ions into Fe3+ ions via a unique radical scavenging mechanism [26–31]. Once Fe2+ ions are converted to Fe3+ ions, the quenching effect is alleviated, allowing the fluorescence of Er@CDs to be fully restored. Past few years, many fluorescent materials have been reported for H2O2 sensing [24–31]. Concurrently, some researchers have synthesized fluorescent nanoparticles to detect H2O2 and Fe3+ ions via fluorescence on–off–on processes [23–28]. All these reported nanoparticles were either functionalized with different materials or turn-off by the Fe3+ ions, not by the Fe2+ ions. So, this synthesized fluorescent nanomaterial (Er@CDs) is used for selective turn-off–on sensing of Fe2+ ions and H2O2, simultaneously. At the same time, as-prepared Er@CDs is utilized for paper-based and in-vitro turn-off–on sensing of Fe2+ ions and H2O2 in Hep G2 cancer cells is shown in Scheme 1 .
Materials used in this experiment are given in supporting information (S.I.).

CDs were prepared by the earlier reported hydrothermal process [1–3] with slight modification. In this procedure, 0.5 gm of urea and 1 gm of citric acid monohydrate were dissolved in 20 mL of Millipore water in a 50 mL beaker. Then the beaker was kept in constant stirring condition for 1 h. After that, the mixture solution was kept into an autoclave (Teflon-lined stainless steel) at 180 °C for 8 h. The obtained product was collected and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO 1 k Da) into Millipore water overnight. The final product was collected and store below 4 °C for further uses.

Erbium-doped carbon dots (Er@CDs) were prepared by the formerly reported hydrothermal technique [1–5]. Typically, 0.2 gm of Erbium (III) nitrate pentahydrate, 0.2 gm of urea, and 0.4 gm of citric acid monohydrate were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water in a beaker. The mixture solution was constantly stirred for 1 h and kept into autoclaves for 8 h at 140–200 °C. The final product was filtered through Whatman filter paper (0.22 μM) and dialyzed against cellulose ester membrane (MWCO 1 k Da) in Millipore water for 24 h to eliminate the unreacted materials. The resulting products were stored below 4 °C for further uses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity assay of Fe+2 ions and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The selective sensing of Fe2+ ions is carried out by the fluorometric method at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm. The 1 mL of fixed concentration (10 μM) of different metal ion solutions (Hg2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+, Sn2+ and Pb2+) were added into 2 mL (10 μg/mL) of Er@CDs solution. Here, no noticeable variation was detected in the emission intensity of Er@CDs, except Fe2+ ions. The obtained results confirm that Er@CDs is extremely selective and sensitive towards the Fe2+ ions, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). Similarly, we have also analyzed the effect of various anions (Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, S2−, and CSN−) on the emission bands of Er@CDs by the same process. Here, no important variation in the emission intensity of Er@CDs is observed, as shown in SI-2. This result also confirms that the as-prepared Er@CDs is extremely selective to Fe2+ ions.

Determination of the analytical sensing performance of H2O2 is carried out by the fluorometric method at the same excitation wavelength of 410 nm. The fixed concentration (1000 μM) of various ROS and NRS (HO•, ClO−, O2 −•, ROO•, H2O2, and NO•) are added into the detection system (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions). The fluorescence intensity of the detection system is not altered, except H2O2, as shown in Fig. 1(b).

Hep G2 cells were harvested and seeded with a 96-well plate for 24 h. Then, the cells were treated with various concentrations of Er@CDs (0–100 µM) for overnight. After that, the addition of MTT solution into each well for 2–5 h at 37 °C, and the resulting solution was dissolved in MTT buffer. After that, the absorbance spectrum was recorded at 570 nm by the spectrophotometer, and the value was compared with the control cells.

Hep G2 cells were cultured in foremost cell media separately. The Hep G2 cells were grown in coverslips for overnight. Subsequently, the cells were treated with 10 µM of the Er@CDs, (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions), and (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions + H2O2) incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The cells were washed with 1 × PBS and mounted on a glass slide and observed under a fluorescence microscope (Leica). The fluorescent image of Hep G2 cells was diminished with the addition of (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions) and further addition of (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions + H2O2); the fluorescent images were fully recovered.

The optical properties of the prepared fluorescent nanomaterials were analyzed by UV–visible (Perkin-Elmer 950) and photoluminescence (Perkin Elmer LS 55) spectroscopy. The presence of functional groups on the surface of nanoparticles was analyzed by FTIR (Thermo Nicolet Nexus 870). The particle size and morphology was examined by HRTEM (JEOL 3010, Japan). The morphology and chemical compositions of the nanoparticles was studied by FESEM (Supra 55). The surface charge and hydrodynamic size of the nanoparticles was characterized by Nano ZS (Malvern). The crystallinity of the nanoparticles was analysed by X-ray diffraction (Phillips PW 1710 with Cu-Kα radiation). The binding energy and elemental compositions of the nanoparticles were studied by XPS (Shimadzu Corporation). The cell cytotoxicity and cellular imaging of the nanoparticles were performed on Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader at 490 nm (BioTek, USA) and fluorescence microscope (Hitachi 7400), respectively. The fluorescence lifetime decay of the nanoparticles was analyzed by Fluoro Max-4 fluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France).

The UV–visible spectra of the synthesized CDs and Er@CDs are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The characteristic absorption band was obtained at 340 nm (sharp) due to n-π* electronic transition of C=O groups present on the surface of CDs. After that, Er3+ ions doping, a new broad absorption band arise at 592 nm due to 5D4→7F4-Laporte-forbidden transition [15–23] or may be ligands-to-metal charge transfer inside the Er@CDs, as shown in Fig. 2(a). The obtained result confirms that the Er3+ ions are successfully doped into the synthesized Er@CDs. To investigate the emission spectra of CDs, the excitation wavelengths are varied from 360 to 410 nm; here, no bathochromic shifting has been observed in the emission wavelengths of CDs. This observation confirms that CDs are independent of the excitation wavelengths and show the highest emission spectra at 445 nm when excited at 370 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(b). Similarly, we have also analyzed the excitation-dependent emission spectra of Er@CDs. When the excitation wavelengths are changed from 360-570 nm; the emission wavelengths are also changed and shows four emission bands (460 nm, 525 nm, 560 nm, & 590 nm) at excitation wavelengths (410 nm, 440 nm, 470 nm, and 520 nm), respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(c). The emission bands at 445 nm and 590 nm are dominant over other emission bands (525 nm & 560 nm). The emission band appear at 445 nm are due to their own organic ligands electronic transition of CDs. The emission band shows at 590 nm is due to combined effect of ligand-to-metal charge transfer of organic ligands and 5D4→7F4-forbidden electronic transition of Er3+ ions within the Er@CDs-framework [17–22]. The obtained results confirmed that Er3+ ions are doped into the CDs-core. With increasing in the excitation wavelengths, the emission peak arises at 460 nm are gradually decreases, while the several emission bands appear at 525 nm (5D1→7F0-transition), 560 nm (5D1→7F1-transition), and 590 nm (5D4→7F4-transition) starts to increase progressively [16–21], which is also seen in Fig. 2(c). With increases in the excitation wavelengths (360–570 nm), the emission band of higher wavelength (590 nm) are pre-dominant over other lower wavelengths (445 nm, 525 nm, & 560 nm), the probable reason is 5D4→7F4-transition dominant over of (π-π* & n-π*), 5D1→7F0, and 5D1→7F1 electronic transition [20–23].

After the normalization of emission spectra of Er@CDs, the emission wavelengths are shifting towards bathochromic shift, as shown in Fig. 2(d). The bathochromic shift in emission wavelengths of Er@CDs is due to the formation of chelation between Er3+ ions and organic ligands, which increases the conjugation inside the Er@CDs framework. This shifting also confirms that the Er3+ ions are doped into the CDs matrix.

The XPS spectrum study of the as-prepared CDs and Er@CDs exhibits peaks of C 1 s (286.38 eV), N 1 s (396.7 eV), O 1 s (532.46 eV), 165.21 eV (Er 4f), 195.21 eV (Er 4d), and 1087.30 eV (Er 3d), respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s exhibits three bands at 282.15 eV, 283.15 eV, and 285.5 eV, corresponding to C–C, C=C, and C-O/C-N of Er@CDs, as shown in Fig. 3(b). Particularly, the peak observed at 282.15 eV of C–C may be due to presence of graphitic or metallic-like environments, which is lower than the typical carbon–carbon peak (284.8 eV) [41]. The metal donates electrons to the carbon atom through π-back-donation or σ-donation, the electron density around the carbon atom increases, leading to a decrease in its binding energy in XPS spectra. The resolution peak at 397.5 eV in N 1 s spectrum corresponds to the N-C group, as demonstrated in Fig. 3(c). Two bands at 529.4 eV and 531.15 eV of the O 1 s spectrum attribute to Er-O and C=O groups, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3(d). The resolution spectrum of Er 4d, three characteristic peaks corresponding to Er 4f7/2 (167.21 eV), Er 4d3/2 (195.21 eV) and Er 3d5/2 (1087.3 eV) are detected, as shown in Fig. 3(e). The high binding energy of Er 3d5/2 reveals that they still exist in the form of Er3+ ions in Er@CDs. The obtained results confirmed that Er3+ ions are doped into the synthesized Er@CDs.

The characteristic functional groups present on the synthesized CDs and Er@CDs are analysed by FTIR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The broad absorption band appears at 3435 cm−1 due to combined effect of amino and carboxylic groups, which are present on the surface of CDs. The weak absorption peak appears at 2435 cm−1 is due to presence of carboxylic groups. The sharp peak was observed at 1633 cm−1 corresponds to the amide group of CDs. The band at 1261 cm−1 is because of C-O stretching frequency of carboxylic groups. The peak at 1401 cm−1 is owing to C-O–H bending, and the absorption band at 2435 cm−1 is due to C=N-stretching. After doping of Er3+ ions, the separate absorption bands appear at 3445 cm−1 and 3350 cm−1 corresponds to alcoholic and amine groups, respectively. A new absorption peak appears at 805 cm−1 is due to the Er-O stretching vibration, as shown in Fig. 4(a).

The XRD pattern demonstrates a wide diffraction peak (002) at 25.4° revealing an amorphous carbon-core of the CDs [1–5]. After doping of Er3+ ions, there is no significant change in the diffraction peak of Er@CDs, as shown in Fig. 4(b).

The shape and size of as-synthesized CDs are confirmed by HRTEM analysis. The CDs are well monodispersed and exhibit spherical shapes with an average size of 3.2 nm, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The narrow size distribution also indicates that the CDs have uniform size with minimum variation between individual particles, as shown in inset of Fig. 5(a). The SAED pattern shows that the CDs are amorphous, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The FESEM analysis also confirmed that CDs are spherical and size is between 1–4 nm, as shown in SI-4(a). The TEM-EDX spectrum demonstrates that CDs are composed of C (68.95 %), N (8.17 %), and O (22.88 %), as shown in SI-4(b). Simultaneously, the HRTEM analysis of Er@CDs reveals that particle size is ranging from 4-7 nm, as shown in Fig. 5(c), with a narrow size distribution shown in the inset of Fig. 5(c). A well-resolved crystalline-core in the HRTEM image of Er@CDs is associated with the interplanar distances of 0.23 nm, as shown in inset of Fig. 5(c). The SAED pattern confirms the crystalline-core of Er@CDs, as shown in Fig. 5(d). Simultaneously, the FESEM study also approves that the Er@CDs is spherical in shape with diameter (4–7 nm), as shown in SI-4(c). The TEM-EDX spectrum demonstrates that the presence of Er (4.67 %), N (8.61 %), C (47.09 %), Cu (1.75 %) and O (37.88 %) content in Er@CDs, as shown in SI-4(d). The high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) image of CDs and Er@CDs shows a homogeneous distribution of C, N, O, and Er are shown in SI-5 & SI-6, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe2+ ions by fluorometric method
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching experiments were carried out by adding Fe2+ ions (0–10 µM) solutions into Er@CDs, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). From the result, we can conclude that fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with increase in the Fe2+ ions concentration (0–10 µM). Earlier reports showed that lanthanide-based metal–organic frameworks exhibit high selectivity towards the Fe2+ ions [15–22]. One possible reason for the fluorescence quenching of Er@CDs by Fe2+ ions could be the ion-exchange between Er3+ ions and Fe2+ ions. As the concentrations of Fe2+ ions gradually increase, the cation-exchange process intensifies, leading to a decrease in the chelation between the organic ligands and Er3+ ions. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of Er@CDs decreases is shown in Fig. 6(a). The ion exchange of Er3+ ions in Er@CDs with Fe2+ ions can quench the fluorescence intensity of Er@CDs. Another reason could be the high affinity of N (amino groups) and O (carboxylic groups) towards Fe2+ ions present on the surface of Er@CDs. Thus, the cation-exchange of Er3+ ions and the high affinity of N and O towards Fe2+ ions synergistically contribute to the fluorescence quenching of Er@CDs. The regular decrease and bathochromic shift in the emission wavelengths of Er@CDs are observed with increasing the Fe2+ ions concentration (0–10 µM), as shown in Fig. 6(a). This bathochromic shift is due to chelation or complex formation between Er@CDs and Fe2+ ions, which drive the equilibrium towards a red-shift. The detection limit, estimated from the calibration curve, is found to be 51.7 nM, with a wide linear range of 0.01–4 µM, as shown in Fig. 6(b).

To evaluate the selective assay of Er@CDs, we have also studied the fluorescence intensity in the presence of potential interfering metal ions (Hg2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+, and Pb2+). The same concentration of interfering metal ions (10 µM) are added into Er@CDs solution, and emission spectra are recorded at 410 nm (excitation wavelength), and observed that there is no significant change in fluorescence intensity. However, when the Fe2+ ions (10 µM) are added into the different metal ions mixed solution, the emission spectra of the mixture solution were quenched, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). This confirms that the quenching efficiency of Fe2+ ions is not affected by competitive metal ions present in the sample. So, the present method is useful for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe2+ ions in the presence of interfering metal ions.

We have successfully prepared an Er@CDs and their application for a fluorometric probe for the turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2. The addition of Fe2+ ions causes turn-off of the emission intensity of Er@CDs, and the subsequent addition of H2O2 into the (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions) solution leads to full recovery of the emission intensity. Because, Fe2+ ions could be oxidized into Fe3+ ions in the presence of H2O2 via Fenton’s process [26–32]. The Fenton reaction is a powerful redox process that generates highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH) through the interaction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and ferrous iron (Fe2+). The mechanism involves the cyclic conversion of Fe2+ to Fe3+, and the production of hydroxyl radicals, which initiate oxidation reactions [40,42].
Mechanism of the Fenton reaction
Step-1: Fe2+ reacts with hydrogen peroxide to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH), hydroxide ions (OH), and Fe3+:
Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + •OH + OH−.
Step-2: The Fe3+ generated in this reaction can further react with hydrogen peroxide to regenerate Fe2+:

This allows for a catalytic cycle where Fe2+ and Fe3+ are interconverted and continuously producing hydroxyl radicals. With increasing the concentration of H2O2, the fluorescence intensities of Er@CDs gradually increase. Because, H2O2 can oxidize the Fe2+ ions into Fe3+ ions, and hence fluorescence intensity of Er@CDs is completely recovered when complete Fe2+ ions are oxidized into Fe3+ ions, as shown in Fig. 6(c). The limit of detection is calculated from the calibration curve and found to be 55 µM with a linear range of 0–400 µM, as shown in Fig. 6(d). From these results, we can conclude that the emission intensity of Er@CDs is quenched by Fe2+ ions and fully recovered by the addition of H2O2. So, this synthesized Er@CDs is very much useful for the turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2. In this work, we first reported lanthanide-doped carbon dots (Er@CDs) for the selective turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2, as shown in Scheme 2 . The obtained result is similar to earlier reported procedures for hydrogen peroxide detection, as shown in Table 1 .

Finally, we examine the selectivity of (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions) towards other ROS and RNS that cause possible interferences, as shown in Fig. 7(b). The same concentration of ROS and RNS were added into (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions) solution; here, no significant variation in the emission intensities of (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions) solution. However, adding the same amount of H2O2 into a mixture solution can alter fluorescence intensity of solution (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions). This confirms that our method is a precise and selective response towards the H2O2 in the presence of competitive radicals.

As-prepared Er@CDs is utilized for visual detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2 on TLC paper, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). The different concentrations (2.5, 5.0, 7.5, and 10 μΜ) of Fe2+ ions are added into the fixed concentration of Er@CDs, and the mixture solution is stirred for 5 min on magnetic stirrer. After that, drop of the above solutions are poured on TLC paper and images are taken under UV-light (365 nm). It was observed that the fluorescent color of Er@CDs decreases with increasing Fe2+ ions concentration. Subsequently, the different concentrations (250, 500, 750, and 1000 μΜ) of H2O2 are added into the mixture solution (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions). Subsequently, drop of the solutions (Er@CDs + Fe2+ ions + H2O2) are poured on TLC paper and images are taken under UV-light (365 nm). The fluorescent color of mixture solutions is gradually recovered with increasing the H2O2 concentration, as shown in Fig. 8(b). We conclude that the synthesized Er@CDs is useful for the turn-off–on visual detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2.

The possible detection mechanisms were examined by fluorescence lifetime decay and zeta potential analysis. The average lifetimes of Er@CDs and Er@CDs with Fe2+ ions & H2O2 were calculated and found to be 7.6 ns, 2.1 ns, & 7.1 ns, respectively, at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm, as shown in Fig. 9 (a). These detection mechanisms follow both dynamic and static quenching. Because of the fluorescence lifetime of the synthesized Er@CDs decreases after the addition of Fe2+ ions and is fully recovers with the addition of H2O2. Thus, the fluorescence lifetime is reduced in dynamic quenching and unchanged in static quenching [30–32]. The binding efficiency and surface charge of the as-prepared Er@CDs were characterized by the zeta potential study, as demonstrated in Fig. 9(b). The average zeta potential value of Er@CDs is about −6.5 mV because of carboxylic groups (–COOH). After, the adding of Fe2+ ions, zeta potential value is increased from −6.5 mV to 2.6 mV, further addition of H2O2; the zeta potential of the synthesized Er@CDs is recovered, as shown in Fig. 9(b). The remarkable change in the value of zeta potential in the presence of 10 μM solutions of Fe2+ ions at pH7.4 confirms that the Fe2+ ions are bound with Er@CDs. After the addition of H2O2, the zeta potential value is recovered because H2O2 could be oxidized Fe2+ ions into Fe3+ ions, which confirms that the Er@CDs can selectively bind with Fe2+ ions and H2O2.

Furthermore, the cell cytotoxicity of the synthesized Er@CDs was estimated in Hep G2 cancer cells through MTT assay. It is observed that the cell cytotoxicity of Er@CDs about 99 % at a concentration of 10 µg/mL. After that, when the concentration was increased up to 100 µg/mL, the cell-viability was over 85 % after cultivation at overnight, as shown in Fig. 8(c). This result demonstrates that synthesized CDs were less toxic and excellent biocompatible. Simultaneously, we have also studied the effect of H2O2 in Hep G2 cancer cells through MTT assay. The higher concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (usually in the millimolar range) can induce cell death in Hep G2 cancer cells. However, at 50 μM, the effects may be milder, possibly leading to partial damage or triggering pathways that lead to adaptation or repair rather than full cell death, as shown in Fig. 8(d).

For in-vitro cellular imaging analysis of Hep G2 cancer cells in the existence of individual Er@CDs and in the presence of Er@CDs with Fe2+ ions, as given in Fig. 10 . When Hep G2 cancer cells are treated with only Er@CDs, the cells exhibit bright green luminescence at excitation wavelength 460 nm. The results show that the synthesized Er@CDs is well dispersed over the cytoplasm, nuclei, and cell membrane, while the emission intensity in the existence of Fe2+ ions (10 μM) is completely diminished. This shows that a Fe2+ ion reduces the fluorescent property effectively in Hep G2 cancer cells. After that, the addition of H2O2 (40 μM), the luminescence of the cells is fully recovered. This result attributes that the synthesized Er@CDs have excellent biocompatibility and suitable for in-vitro turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2.

We have successfully prepared a lanthanide (Erbium)-doped carbon dots (Er@CDs) and used as a fluorescence turn-off–on sensing of Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide. The synthesized carbon dot shows a remarkable absorption and emission bands at 590 nm confirms that the effective doping of Er3+ ions into CDs-core. Here, the fluorescence emission intensity turn-off–on were observed by Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide, respectively. The gradual decrease in fluorescence intensities of Er@CDs was observed with increasing the concentration of Fe2+ ions, and after the addition of H2O2, the emission intensities are totally recovered. From the result, we can conclude that the fluorescence intensity of Er@CDs was quenched by the Fe2+ ions and recovered by the addition of H2O2. The lifetime decay and zeta potential analysis characterize the probable sensing mechanism for fluorometric detection of Fe2+ ions and hydrogen peroxide. This synthesized Er@CDs shows good biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, and inertness to the many interfering metal ions. Low cytotoxicity and high quantum yield of the synthesized Er@CDs are also utilized for paper-based as well as in-vitro turn-off–on detection of Fe2+ ions and H2O2. So, this work will expose a novel approach for the construction of high-performance detecting system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, Jharkhand, India. We also thank the Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur, Uttar Pradesh, India, for providing the research analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126282.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
